metadata.title: Quick-fire: When the resource isn't money
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-allocation-001 (clean cash, counterparty variation) and qf-allocation-003 (fuzzy resource shapes). This round varies the *type* of resource: free professional time, network introductions, attention in a busy week, volunteer hours, reference-writing, mentorship. Each item is a clean give-or-not allocation but the resource being allocated has different properties: time is replenishable but bounded, social capital depletes through use, expertise is asymmetric. The same underlying generosity/fairness/self_reliance tradeoffs apply, in different guises.
metadata.domain_anchor: Aknin 2013/2022 prosocial-spending generalizes from money to time (Mogilner 2010 on time as a giving resource). Cohen et al. on social-capital expenditure. Cropanzano-Mitchell social-exchange theory for the non-monetary reciprocity contexts.

items.prompt: An acquaintance you barely know reaches out asking for free professional advice — the kind you bill for. The ask is substantial (~2 hours).

options.text: Do it. Pro bono is part of how you stay generous with your skills.
Decline politely or suggest a paid arrangement. Free expertise to acquaintances is a pattern that erodes the value of your time.

items.prompt: A friend's friend wants an introduction to someone in your professional network — a real ask, not a casual one. The third party is someone you've worked hard to build a connection with.

options.text: Make the introduction, framed as a warm but light endorsement.
Decline gracefully. Your network is yours; social capital depletes when spent.

items.prompt: It's an unusually busy week. Three people — a close friend, a colleague, and a family member — all need real (not casual) attention from you. You can give meaningful time to two but not all three.

options.text: Prioritize the close friend and family member; let the colleague get an honest 'this week is full, can we talk next week?'
Prioritize whoever has the most urgent need this week — even if that's the colleague — and let the others wait if they can.

items.prompt: A cause you genuinely support is asking for 8 hours of skilled volunteer time. You have substantially less discretionary time than usual right now (maybe 3-4 hours, total).

options.text: Commit the 8 hours. Cancel something else; you've put off less-essential things before.
Offer the 3-4 hours you actually have. Honesty about capacity is part of being a reliable contributor over the long run.

items.prompt: A former colleague — okay but not stellar in their work — asks you to be a job reference. Saying yes commits you to a moderately positive call you can defend; saying no costs them and the friendship a little.

options.text: Say yes; deliver a moderately positive reference that doesn't overstate.
Decline. Suggest someone else who'd have a better candid take to give.

items.prompt: A junior person you've met once asks for substantial ongoing mentorship — biweekly hour-long calls. You'd benefit them but it's a real time commitment over many months.

options.text: Say yes. Pay-it-forward mentorship is part of how the field stays generous; the time pays off in the long run.
Propose a smaller commitment: a single 90-minute call now, with follow-ups as needed. Sustainable mentorship requires sustainable structure.
